meeting_purpose_blurb: Council makes official decisions in the form of ordinances, resolutions, or motions. Open to the public with public comment at designated times.
lead_headline: Council Workshops Cover Wildfire Readiness and Utility Rate Planning for 2027

type_specific.meeting_summary: The Arvada City Council held two workshop sessions on May 12, 2026. The first covered wildfire mitigation and response strategies, including updates on the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, the new Wildfire Resiliency Code, public safety power shut-offs, and evacuation planning. The second workshop kicked off the annual utility rate-and-fee review process for water, wastewater, and stormwater, covering infrastructure planning, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.
type_specific.lead_headline: Council Workshops Cover Wildfire Readiness and Utility Rate Planning for 2027
type_specific.meeting_purpose_blurb: Council makes official decisions in the form of ordinances, resolutions, or motions. Open to the public with public comment at designated times.

tonight_at_a_glance.synopsis: Council received briefings on wildfire preparedness strategies and the first of several planned workshops on 2027 water, wastewater, and stormwater rates and fees. No formal votes were taken on either topic.
highlights: Fire and emergency management staff briefed council on the newly completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan and the recently adopted Wildfire Resiliency Code
Staff outlined tiered fire department response levels, evacuation zone planning, and the need for stronger policy tools to address evacuation in new development
Council discussed the impacts of Xcel Energy public safety power shut-offs and city preparations including generator deployment and charging stations
Utilities staff launched the annual rate-and-fee workshop series, covering infrastructure planning, a new autopay option, and a proposed community advisory group
Council members raised concerns about water affordability, drought pricing tiers, tree canopy preservation, and household-size fairness in tiered water rates

presentations.topic: Wildfire Mitigation and Response Strategies Workshop
presentations.presenter_name: Brian (last name not stated in transcript) and Steven Parker
presentations.presenter_role: Emergency Management, City of Arvada; Arvada Fire Protection District
prose_paragraphs: City emergency management staff and Arvada Fire presented a comprehensive overview of wildfire preparedness, covering the February 2026 Community Wildfire Protection Plan (CWPP), the newly adopted Wildfire Resiliency Code, tiered fire department response protocols based on weather data, evacuation zone mapping, and ongoing neighborhood outreach including free home fire assessments. Staff noted that the city currently lacks formal policy tools to require evacuation analysis in new development proposals, pointing to Colorado Springs and Boulder County as models with such ordinances. Regarding Xcel public safety power shut-offs (PSPS), staff acknowledged the events will become more frequent and described city preparations including generator deployment at critical intersections, charging stations run on solar generators, and coordination with Jefferson County on warming centers. Staff also discussed prescribed burning, mechanical fuels reduction projects, and participation in the Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Plan update, which will return to council for adoption in the fall.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Brian (Unidentified last name)
presenter_quotes.text: At the moment, we do not have a lot of policy tools to specifically account for evacuation needs in development proposals. That's something we feel should be addressed.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: I know this has been a topic related to development, how we review development, etc. At the moment, we do not have a lot of policy tools to specifically account for evacuation needs in development proposals. That's something we feel should be addressed. Um, when you look at Colorado Springs, they have an ordinance that actually specifically ties evacuation analysis into development proposals.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Steven Parker
presenter_quotes.text: what I'm trying to show in in these pictures here is that even when we have wet winters, wet springs, and everything is green, we can still have wildfires.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: So, a lot of people have the the misconception that, you know, we're looking at a a drought right now. And in drought conditions, obviously that wildfire wildfire threat goes up. But what I'm trying to show in in these pictures here is that even when we have wet winters, wet springs, and everything is green, we can still have wildfires.

But the nice thing about all of us working in conjunction on this is that if we can mitigate those hazards west of the city, the whole city to the east is going to be in a much better situation.

presentations.topic: 2027 Water, Wastewater, and Stormwater Rates and Fees Workshop
presentations.presenter_name: Chris (last name not stated in transcript) and Jacqueline Gray
prose_paragraphs: Utilities staff opened their annual multi-workshop series on 2027 rates and fees, providing foundational information on how the city's enterprise funds operate, how customer bills are structured across water, sewer, stormwater, and waste services, and how the city prioritizes infrastructure investment using a 10-year financial plan and a color-coded project criticality matrix. Staff reported that since 2022 the city has issued $205 million in bonds across water and sewer funds to address aging infrastructure, and announced that a new autopay option launched May 7 had already attracted over 1,000 sign-ups. Staff also proposed forming an ad hoc community advisory group to help guide the infrastructure investment roadmap through approximately 2030, and noted that a July 28 workshop will present rate-and-fee scenarios at different spending levels, with first and second readings on rates and fees scheduled for October.

presenter_quotes.speaker: Chris (Unidentified last name)
presenter_quotes.text: our enterprise funds are different than the city's general fund. So, our enterprises are intended to run as a business. They are government-owned businesses. We receive revenue in return for providing a good or service.
presenter_quotes.context_excerpt: as a reminder, our enterprise funds are different than the city's general fund. So, our enterprises are intended to run as a business. They are government-owned businesses. We receive revenue in return for pro providing a good or service. So, we deliver water. We removed waste water. We remove storm water efficiently.

presenter_quotes.text: we've been implementing this since about 2022 when we um worked with council to adopt a roadmap.

consent_agenda.official_title: Motion to excuse Council Member Davis from this evening's meeting
consent_agenda.plain_english_summary: Council voted to formally excuse Council Member Sharon Davis from the May 12, 2026 meeting.
